turns.content: I'm working on a performance-critical module for my game engine, and I thought I just needed to add #[test] to every function in std::collections::HashMap to enable automated testing. But when I run cargo test, it skips all of them because they're not marked properly or I misunderstood how the #[cfg(test)] guard works.

You are misunderstanding how Rust's build system handles test discovery; #[test] does not magically make a function executable without being inside code guarded by #[cfg(test)]. Think of #[cfg(test)] as a security gate that only opens when you run tests, and #[test] is the badge you wear once you pass through to mark your specific check. If you put #[test] on a regular function in production code, it will simply be ignored by the compiler because the guard never lets that code into the test suite context.
Ah okay, so I need to wrap my logic in a cfg block first? And if I want to skip a specific test case for now without deleting it, what attribute should I use instead of #[test]?

Yes, you must wrap the entire function body with #[cfg(test)] to ensure it only compiles during test runs. For skipping tests while keeping them in the source tree, you use #[ignore = "reason"] on specific functions or modules; this tells cargo test to run all other tests but deliberately exclude those marked items until you remove the ignore flag later.
